ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific class for cream-coated biscuit sticks; 'biscuit food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits and cookies — sweet, cream-filled or coated
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN0AN covers sweet biscuits and cookies; cream-coated stick format is not separately enumerated. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for cream-coated biscuit sticks; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is approximately 20 g (roughly 5–6 biscuit sticks), as commonly sold in individual snack packs in Singapore supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Biscuit stick coated with cream (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Biscuit stick coated with cream (any flavour)', CR ID F11080003. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from this record. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 11 g/100g based on typical palm-oil cream coating composition consistent with HPB category data; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat set to -1 as HPB does not report these sub-fractions for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cream-coated biscuit sticks in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 74 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain sweet biscuits / cookies made from refined wheat flour (GI ~70–74, University of Sydney GI Database). The cream coating adds sugar but does not substantially alter the GI relative to the biscuit base. Glycemic load per 20 g serving = (74 × 13.08 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 9.7. Available carbohydrate = carbohydrate_g minus dietary_fibre_g = (65.42 − 1.14) × 0.20 = 12.86 g; GL = (74 × 12.86) / 100 ≈ 9.5, rounded to 9.7 using total carbohydrate basis of 65.42 × 0.20 = 13.08 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (30.18 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.14 g/100g) triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (~11 g/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this snack: the high sugar content (30.18 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention per AHA 2018 guidance, and the saturated fat from palm-oil cream coating promotes arterial stiffness, both of which elevate blood pressure risk independent of the moderate sodium level (174 mg/100g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 30.18 g/100g far exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling; high GI (~74) and high carbohydrate (65.42 g/100g) with negligible fibre (1.14 g/100g) create extreme postprandial glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this snack entirely: a single 20 g serving delivers approximately 6 g of sugar with negligible fibre buffering, producing a high glycaemic load (GL ~9.7) that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose spikes; replace with unsalted nuts, seeds, or vegetable sticks with hummus.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~11 g/100g from palm-oil cream coating) raises LDL cholesterol; high sugar (30.18 g/100g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and systemic inflammation; ultra-processed formulation with likely trans fat residuals
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this snack: the high saturated fat content (~11 g/100g) raises LDL cholesterol, the high sugar load (30.18 g/100g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial inflammation, and the ultra-processed nature of the product is independently associated with increased cardiovascular mortality per PREDIMED and NutriNet-Santé cohort data.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~11 g/100g) exceeds 5 g/100g RA threshold; sugar (30.18 g/100g) exceeds 15 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; palm oil does not qualify for MUFA/PUFA carve-out
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this snack: the combination of high saturated fat (~11 g/100g from palm-oil coating) and high sugar (30.18 g/100g) strongly promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and the palm-oil base does not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA anti-inflammatory carve-out.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Biscuit stick coated with cream (any flavour) is an ultra-processed snack food consisting of thin, elongated baked wheat biscuit sticks that are partially or fully dipped in a flavoured cream coating, typically chocolate, strawberry, or vanilla. The biscuit base is made from refined wheat flour, sugar, vegetable fat, and leavening agents, baked to a crisp, light texture. The cream coating is formulated from sugar, palm-based vegetable fat, milk solids, and flavouring agents, producing a sweet, slightly waxy outer layer that contrasts with the crunchy biscuit interior. This product is widely sold in Singapore and across Southeast Asia in individual snack packs of approximately 20 g. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 495 kcal/100g, with high total fat (22.99 g/100g, predominantly saturated from palm oil at ~11 g/100g), very high sugar (30.18 g/100g), and high refined carbohydrate (65.42 g/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (1.14 g/100g). The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 74 (high), producing a glycaemic load of ~9.7 per 20 g serving. This snack is clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar, saturated fat, and ultra-processed composition. It is best consumed only occasionally and in small portions by healthy individuals.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080003), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All key nutrients are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat is estimated from typical palm-oil cream coating composition consistent with HPB category data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest approved equivalent (sweet biscuits, refined wheat flour base, GI ~74).
notes: 1. Saturated fat (~11 g/100g) is estimated based on typical palm-oil cream coating composition for this product category; HPB does not report saturated fat sub-fractions separately for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin fields set to -1: detailed fatty acid sub-fractions, micronutrient, and vitamin data for this product are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. calcium_mg reported as 0.0 and iron_mg reported as 0.0 per HPB source data (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F11080003). 4. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source for this entry. 5. GI of 74 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using sweet biscuits (refined wheat flour) as the closest approved equivalent; no direct GI measurement exists for cream-coated biscuit sticks. 6. The 'wholegrain' dietary tag present in the input record has been removed as it is inconsistent with the product composition (refined wheat flour base); this is flagged for source review. 7. Trans fat is listed as approximately 0.3 g/100g in the input health_suitability narrative; however, this value is not confirmed in the HPB per_100g data and has been set to -1 pending source verification.
